originalTitle: Sexual violence increasingly used as 'weapon of war' in Sudan, UN says
title: Helpers in Sudan: Making Homes Safe Again!
content:
Hello, little explorers and big thinkers from all over our amazing continent of Africa! Your friendly news reporter is here, ready to share a story from a country called Sudan. Sudan is a big country in Africa, just like many of our homes are big and full of people. Sometimes, things happen in the world that are a bit sad, but it's important to learn about them and know that there are always helpers trying to make things better.

What's Happening in Sudan?

Imagine your home is usually a happy place where you play, learn, and feel safe. But sometimes, in Sudan, grown-ups are having big fights, almost like a very serious argument that has turned into people fighting with armies. These fights make it hard for people to feel safe in their homes and communities. It's not like a small fight in the playground; it's much bigger and can make many people sad and scared.

Did you know? Sudan is the third-largest country in Africa! It has a very long river called the Nile running through it.

When Homes Don't Feel Safe

When there's fighting, sometimes very bad things happen. People are meant to be safe, especially in their own homes and villages. But sadly, some grown-ups who are fighting in Sudan are doing very unkind things to other people. They are hurting people in ways that are never okay. They do these bad things to make everyone feel very, very scared. It's like they want to scare people so much that they give up or run away from their homes.

Imagine if someone tried to make you afraid just so they could win a game. That's not fair, right? Well, these people are trying to make others afraid to win their fight. This is called using 'fear as a weapon'. It means they are using scary and hurtful actions, not just guns, to hurt people and make them feel unsafe.

Did you know? A 'community' means all the people who live near each other, like your neighbourhood, your village, or your town. Everyone in a community helps each other!

Who Are the UN Helpers?

There's a very special big group of helpers in the world called the United Nations, or the UN for short. Imagine if all the good teachers, doctors, and wise grown-ups from almost every country in the world came together to help. That's a bit like the UN! They want everyone in the world to be safe, happy, and live in peace.

The UN helpers are watching what is happening in Sudan. They see the bad things that are making people scared and hurt. They talk about it so that everyone in the world knows, and they try to get the fighting to stop. They also try to help the people who are hurt and scared, giving them food, water, and safe places to stay if they have to leave their homes.

Did you know? The UN has a special group called UNICEF that works just to help children all around the world!

Why This Is Important for Kids

When bad things happen in a country, it can make life very difficult for everyone, especially for children. If people are scared to be in their homes, or if they have to leave, it means they might miss school. Missing school means missing out on learning, playing with friends, and doing fun things. It can be very sad and confusing for children when their normal life suddenly changes.

Children in Sudan want to play games, go to school, laugh with their families, and dream about their future, just like you! But when fighting and fear are around, it makes it hard to do these things. It's very important that everyone, especially children, feels safe and protected.

Did you know? Playing games helps children learn important things like sharing, teamwork, and how to solve problems!

Working for Peace and Safety

Even though this news is sad, remember there are many, many good people working very hard to make things better. The UN helpers, and many other kind people and groups, are talking to the people who are fighting. They are saying, "Please stop the fighting! Let's find a way to make peace." They want everyone in Sudan to be safe and for the bad things to stop.

They are also helping people who have been hurt to get better. They are trying to make sure that children can go back to school and play again. It's a bit like when you skin your knee, and someone helps you clean it and put a plaster on it – these helpers are trying to heal a whole country!

Did you know? When people work together and talk instead of fight, it's called 'peacebuilding'. It's a very important job!

What We Can Do

So, what can we do, even though we are far away? We can think about the children and families in Sudan and wish for peace and safety for them. We can also remember to be extra kind to our friends and family, to share, and to always try to solve our own little arguments with words instead of fighting. Because when we are kind and peaceful in our own communities, we help make the whole world a better place!

Let's hope that soon, all the homes in Sudan will be filled with safety, laughter, and happiness again. Keep exploring, keep learning, and keep being kind, my friends!
summary: Bad things are happening in Sudan where people are being hurt and made to feel scared to win a fight, but special helpers from the UN are working hard to make everyone safe and bring peace back to homes.
whyMatters: This matters because everyone, especially children, deserves to feel safe in their homes and communities, and when people are hurting others to make them afraid, it stops them from living happy and peaceful lives.

originalTitle: Zambia ex-president's family wins latest legal battle over what should happen to his body
title: Zambia ex-president's family wins latest legal battle over what should happen to his body
content: Edgar Lungu's family and the Zambian government remained in dispute 12 months after he died.

content: Niger, along with Mali and Burkina Faso, had called the ICC an "instrument of neo-colonialist repression".

content: The resolution is largely symbolic, but it adds to pressure on the White House to end the conflict once and for all.

originalTitle: Drowning deaths soar in France as Europe buckles in peak of heatwave
title: Drowning deaths soar in France as Europe buckles in peak of heatwave
content: Forty people have drowned in heatwave-related deaths in France since last Thursday, Prime Minister Sébastien Lecornu says.

content: US Secretary of State Marco Rubio warned Iran that no country can charge fees for ships to travel through the strait.

title: No need to panic for England - but this was a reality check
content: England's drab goalless draw with Ghana is no cause to panic - but it does serve as a reality check, writes Phil McNulty.

originalTitle: 'VAR went for a coffee' - should Ghana have had penalty against England?
title: 'VAR went for a coffee' - should Ghana have had penalty against England?
content: England fans have been left frustrated by the goalless draw against Ghana, but were Thomas Tuchel's side fortunate not to have given away a penalty?

title: Silly tackle, bad reaction - Tuchel defends Bellingham after Queiroz row
